items.q: What is the doctrine of the Trinity?
items.correct: God is one being existing in three persons (Father, Son, Holy Spirit) — distinct in person but united in essence; Christians remain monotheistic.
items.misconception: Christians believe in three different gods.
items.random_wrong: The Trinity refers to heaven, earth and hell.
items.why: Students treat 'three persons' as 'three gods'. The orthodox position is one God in three distinct persons sharing one substance — a notoriously difficult concept students often misrepresent as polytheism.

items.q: What does the Abrahamic covenant in Judaism involve?
items.correct: God's promise to Abraham of land, descendants and blessing, in return for faithfulness — the foundational covenant marking Jews as a covenant people with responsibilities, not privileges.
items.misconception: Being 'chosen' means Jews believe they are superior to other peoples.
items.random_wrong: The covenant means Jews must not interact with non-Jews.
items.why: Chosenness in Jewish theology means chosen for responsibility (following Torah, being a 'light to the nations'), not chosen for favour. Students misread it as ethnic supremacy, missing the obligation it imposes.

items.correct: Five distinct arguments for God's existence: from motion, causation, contingency, gradation of being, and final causes/teleology — collectively forming Aquinas's natural theology, not a single argument.
items.misconception: Aquinas's Five Ways are five steps of one cosmological argument.
items.random_wrong: Aquinas's Five Ways are five Christian sacraments.
items.why: Each Way is a self-standing argument with different structure. Students treat them as a single chain, missing that Aquinas offered multiple independent routes — the failure of one doesn't undermine the others.

items.q: In what year did same-sex marriage become legal in England and Wales?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 2014. “2010” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Qur'an 4:3 permits up to how many wives, on condition of equal treatment?
items.why: It's Four. “Two” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Maximum number of wives permitted in Qur'an 4:3 (on condition of equal treatment)?
items.why: The real figure is 4. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
